# Kafka Consumer Offset Commit to GroupCoordinator Chain

## Your Task

Trace how a consumer offset commit travels from KafkaConsumer.commitSync() to the __consumer_offsets topic in apache/kafka. Find Java source files implementing: commitSync(), ConsumerCoordinator.commitOffsetsSync(), GroupCoordinator.handleCommitOffsets(), and the offset log append.

## Context

You are working on a codebase task involving repos from the crossrepo domain.

## Available Resources

The local `/workspace/` directory contains: sg-evals/kafka--0753c489, sg-evals/flink--0cc95fcc, sg-evals/camel--1006f047.

**Note:** Additional repositories are accessible via Sourcegraph MCP tools:
- `sg-evals/kafka--0753c489` (apache/kafka)
- `sg-evals/flink--0cc95fcc` (apache/flink)
- `sg-evals/camel--1006f047` (apache/camel)

## Output Format

Create a file at `/workspace/answer.json` with your findings in the following structure:

```json
{
  "files": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go"}
  ],
  "symbols": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "SymbolName"}
  ],
  "chain": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "FunctionName"}
  ],
  "text": "Narrative explanation of your findings, citing repos and file paths."
}
```

Include only the fields relevant to this task. Your answer is evaluated against a closed-world oracle — completeness matters.

## Evaluation

Your answer will be scored on:
- **File recall and precision**: Did you find all relevant files?
I'll help you trace the Kafka consumer offset commit chain. Let me start by exploring the repository structure and finding the relevant source files.

74- * Note that offsets are not guaranteed to be consecutive (such as compacted topic or when records have been produced 75- * using transactions). For example, if the consumer did read a record with offset 4, but 5 is not an offset 76- * with a record, its position might advance to 6 (or higher) directly. Similarly, if the consumer's position is 5, 77- * but there is no record with offset 5, the consumer will return the record with the next higher offset. 78- * There are actually two notions of position relevant to the user of the consumer: 79- *

80- * The {@link #position(TopicPartition) position} of the consumer gives the offset of the next record that will be given 81- * out. It will be one larger than the highest offset the consumer has seen in that partition. It automatically advances 82- * every time the consumer receives messages in a call to {@link #poll(Duration)}. 83- *

84: * The {@link #commitSync() committed position} is the last offset that has been stored securely. Should the 85- * process fail and restart, this is the offset that the consumer will recover to. The consumer can either automatically commit 86- * offsets periodically; or it can choose to control this committed position manually by calling one of the commit APIs 87: * (e.g. {@link #commitSync() commitSync} and {@link #commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback) commitAsync}). 88- *

89- * This distinction gives the consumer control over when a record is considered consumed. It is discussed in further 90- * detail below. 91- * 92- *

Consumer Groups and Topic Subscriptions

93- * 94- * Kafka uses the concept of consumer groups to allow a pool of processes to divide the work of consuming and 95- * processing records. These processes can either be running on the same machine or they can be 96- * distributed over many machines to provide scalability and fault tolerance for processing. All consumer instances 97- * sharing the same {@code group.id} will be part of the same consumer group. -- 135- * will stay in the group and continue to receive messages from the partitions it was assigned. Underneath the covers, 136- * the consumer sends periodic heartbeats to the server. If the consumer crashes or is unable to send heartbeats for 137- * a duration of {@code session.timeout.ms}, then the consumer will be considered dead and its partitions will 138- * be reassigned. 139- *

140- * It is also possible that the consumer could encounter a "livelock" situation where it is continuing 141- * to send heartbeats, but no progress is being made. To prevent the consumer from holding onto its partitions 142- * indefinitely in this case, we provide a liveness detection mechanism using the {@code max.poll.interval.ms} 143- * setting. Basically if you don't call poll at least as frequently as the configured max interval, 144- * then the client will proactively leave the group so that another consumer can take over its partitions. When this happens, 145: * you may see an offset commit failure (as indicated by a {@link CommitFailedException} thrown from a call to {@link #commitSync()}). 146- * This is a safety mechanism which guarantees that only active members of the group are able to commit offsets. 147- * So to stay in the group, you must continue to call poll. 148- *

149- * The consumer provides two configuration settings to control the behavior of the poll loop: 150- *

    151- *
  1. max.poll.interval.ms: By increasing the interval between expected polls, you can give 152- * the consumer more time to handle a batch of records returned from {@link #poll(Duration)}. The drawback 153- * is that increasing this value may delay a group rebalance since the consumer will only join the rebalance 154- * inside the call to poll. You can use this setting to bound the time to finish a rebalance, but 155- * you risk slower progress if the consumer cannot actually call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} often enough.

When we have enough records 241- * batched, we will insert them into a database. If we allowed offsets to auto commit as in the previous example, records 242- * would be considered consumed after they were returned to the user in {@link #poll(Duration) poll}. It would then be 243- * possible 244- * for our process to fail after batching the records, but before they had been inserted into the database. -- 250- * would consume from last committed offset and would repeat the insert of the last batch of data. Used in this way 251- * Kafka provides what is often called "at-least-once" delivery guarantees, as each record will likely be delivered one 252- * time but in failure cases could be duplicated. 253- *

    254- * Note: Using automatic offset commits can also give you "at-least-once" delivery, but the requirement is that 255- * you must consume all data returned from each call to {@link #poll(Duration)} before any subsequent calls, or before 256- * {@link #close() closing} the consumer. If you fail to do either of these, it is possible for the committed offset 257- * to get ahead of the consumed position, which results in missing records. The advantage of using manual offset 258- * control is that you have direct control over when a record is considered "consumed." 259- *

I've successfully traced the Kafka consumer offset commit chain from `KafkaConsumer.commitSync()` to the `__consumer_offsets` topic. Here's what I found:

## Complete Call Chain

**Client Side (Consumer):**
1. `KafkaConsumer.commitSync()` - Entry point in the public API
2. `ClassicKafkaConsumer.commitSync()` - Actual implementation that calls coordinator
3. `ConsumerCoordinator.commitOffsetsSync()` - Synchronous commit logic
4. `ConsumerCoordinator.sendOffsetCommitRequest()` - Creates and sends the network request

**Server Side (Broker Group Coordinator):**
5. `GroupCoordinatorService.commitOffsets()` - Receives the network request
6. `GroupCoordinatorShard.commitOffset()` - Processes the offset commit
7. `OffsetMetadataManager.commitOffset()` - Main server-side handler that validates and processes the commit
8. `GroupCoordinatorRecordHelpers.newOffsetCommitRecord()` - Creates the actual CoordinatorRecord to be logged

**Log Persistence:**
9. `CoordinatorRuntime.CoordinatorWriteEvent.run()` - Executes the write operation
10. `CoordinatorRuntime.CoordinatorContext.append()` - Batches records
11. `CoordinatorRuntime.flushCurrentBatch()` - Flushes batch when full or linger timeout expires
12. `PartitionWriter.append()` - Appends MemoryRecords to the `__consumer_offsets` topic
